meta.subject: physics
meta.topic_name: Sticking of Person With Wall of Rotor

intro.kicker: Topic 19 of 22 • Chapter: Friction • Unit: Mechanics • Physics
intro.topicName: Sticking of Person With Wall of Rotor
intro.description: A person stands against the inner wall of a cylindrical drum (rotor) rotating about a vertical axis. Centrifugal force (mω²r) presses the person outward against the wall, providing the normal reaction N = mω²r. The resulting friction f = μN = μmω²r acts upward to support the person’s weight. When μmω²r ≥ mg, the floor can be removed and the person remains stuck to the wall. The critical condition gives the minimum angular velocity: ω_min = √(g/μr). This is a standard NEET application combining circular motion, centrifugal force, and friction.

expectedQuestions.description: NEET direct numerical: given μ, r, and g, find minimum ω (or minimum Revolution Per Second n = ω/2π). Also asked as: what is the minimum speed v_min = ωr for the person not to fall? (v_min = √(gr/μ).) Conceptual variant: does ω_min depend on the person’s mass? (No.) or on the radius? (Yes: ω_min decreases with increasing r.)

timeRequired.description: 30 min to build the FBD and derive ω_min, 15 min on NEET MCQs. The key concept: centrifugal force provides horizontal normal reaction (perpendicular to wall), and friction provides vertical support. This is the same FBD orientation as the cart-block problem but driven by rotation instead of linear acceleration.

difficulty.description: The conceptual challenge is connecting circular motion (ω²r) to the friction equilibrium. Students strong in circular motion but not in friction applications may forget that the friction must equal mg. Another trap: using linear acceleration a = v²/r instead of ω²r when deriving N. Both give the same N = mv²/r = mω²r, but must be consistent in the final formula.

nriUsaGap.description: The ‘death cylinder’ or rotor ride is a NEET-specific application of circular motion + friction. AP Physics teaches both circular motion and friction but not their combination in this particular real-world context. NRI students should practise the FBD: centrifugal force outward (non-inertial frame) → normal reaction by wall inward → friction force upward. The result ω_min = √(g/μr) must be memorised.

weightage.sectionTitle: Sticking of Person With Wall of Rotor — NEET Weightage Analysis (2019–2024)
bulletNotes: Appears every alternate year; 1 question per appearance.
ω_min = √(g/μr) is the formula; also asked as v_min = √(gr/μ).
Conceptual question: does ω_min increase or decrease with larger radius r? (Decreases.)

strategySteps.title: Draw the FBD in the Rotating Frame
strategySteps.description: In the rotor frame (non-inertial): centrifugal force mω²r acts outward (radially outward), pressing person against outer wall. Normal reaction N = mω²r (inward, from wall). Friction f = μN = μmω²r acts upward. Weight mg downward. Equilibrium: μmω²r ≥ mg ⇒ ω ≥ √(g/μr).

strategySteps.title: Derive v_min and ω_min
strategySteps.description: ω_min = √(g/μr). Linear speed: v_min = ω_min × r = r√(g/μr) = √(gr/μ). Knowing both forms is NEET-important since some problems give v and others give ω.

strategySteps.title: Check Dependence on r
strategySteps.description: ω_min = √(g/μr): larger radius ⇒ smaller minimum angular velocity. v_min = √(gr/μ): larger radius ⇒ larger minimum linear speed. This counterintuitive r-dependence is frequently tested as a conceptual NEET question.

cards.title: Rotor Sticking Problem Notes
cards.description: FBD in rotating frame, derivation of ω_min = √(g/μr) and v_min = √(gr/μ), comparison with cart-block problem, radius-dependence discussion, and 6 solved NEET-pattern MCQs.

cards.title: Circular Motion + Friction Summary
cards.description: Unified summary of rotor sticking, banked-road friction, and conical pendulum — all applications combining circular motion with friction or normal force analysis.

subtopicsIndex.sectionTitle: Subtopics in Sticking of Person With Wall of Rotor

cards.title: Rotor Minimum Speed Derivation
cards.coreRevision: Person of mass m inside rotor of radius r rotating at ω (rad/s). In rotating frame: centrifugal force F_c = mω²r (outward). Normal reaction N = mω²r (by wall, inward). Friction f = μN = μmω²r (upward). Condition: f ≥ mg ⇒ μmω²r ≥ mg ⇒ ω² ≥ g/(μr) ⇒ ω_min = √(g/μr).
points: N = mω²r (centrifugal force provides normal reaction)
Mass m cancels: ω_min independent of mass

curriculum.hintText: How AP Physics compares to NEET on the rotor sticking problem.

gaps.title: Rotor Application Not Standard in AP Physics
gaps.description: AP Physics studies circular motion and friction independently but does not combine them in the rotor/death-cylinder context. The formula ω_min = √(g/μr) and the FBD with vertical friction opposing gravity in a rotating system are NEET-specific applications that must be practised explicitly.
bullets: Centrifugal force in rotating frame provides N; f = μN must support mg.
r-dependence: ω_min ∝ 1/√r; v_min ∝ √r. Know both trends.

questions.question: A 60 kg person stands inside a cylindrical rotor of radius 2 m. If μ = 0.5, the minimum angular velocity so that the person does not fall (g = 10 m/s²) is:
questions.explanation: ω_min = √(g/μr) = √(10/(0.5×2)) = √(10/1) = √10 rad/s. Note: mass (60 kg) does not affect ω_min — it cancels from both sides of the equilibrium equation.

questions.question: For the rotor problem, what happens to minimum angular velocity when the radius of the rotor is increased to 4r?
questions.explanation: ω_min = √(g/μr). If r → 4r: ω_min’ = √(g/(4μr)) = ω_min/2. Increasing radius by 4x reduces minimum angular velocity to half.

questions.question: For the rotor (μ = 0.4, r = 1 m, g = 10 m/s²), v_min equals:
ω_min for a rotor depends on which of the following?
For the same μ, a smaller radius rotor requires a ______ minimum angular velocity:
In the rotor problem, what provides the normal reaction on the person?
options: The cylindrical wall (via centrifugal force pressing)
questions.answer: The cylindrical wall (via centrifugal force pressing)

items.title: Memorise ω_min and v_min formulas
items.description: ω_min = √(g/μr) and v_min = √(gr/μ). Both are mass-independent. Know when to use ω form vs v form based on what the problem gives.

items.title: Understand centrifugal force as source of N
items.description: N = mω²r is the centrifugal (pseudo) force pressing the person against the wall. Friction = μN acts vertically upward against weight. This FBD must be sketched correctly in exams.

items.title: Know the r-dependence
items.description: ω_min ∝ 1/√r: larger rotor ⇒ lower required ω. v_min ∝ √r: larger rotor ⇒ higher required linear speed. Both results should be verified by inspection of the formulas.

items.question: Why does the minimum angular velocity not depend on the person’s mass?
items.answer: The equilibrium condition is μmω²r = mg. Dividing both sides by m gives μω²r = g, so ω_min = √(g/μr). The mass m cancels completely. Both the centrifugal force (pressing the person outward) and the person’s weight are proportional to m, so their ratio is mass-independent.

items.question: What is the real-world example of this problem?
items.answer: The ‘Death Cylinder’ or ‘Rotor Ride’ in amusement parks works on this principle. The drum spins fast enough that friction between the rough drum wall and riders’ clothing/bodies supports them against gravity. Once the ride reaches ω_min, the floor can be lowered and riders remain pinned to the wall.

items.question: How is this problem different from the cart-block sticking problem?
items.answer: In the cart-block problem, the horizontal pressing force is provided by linear acceleration (pseudo-force ma in the cart’s frame). In the rotor problem, the horizontal pressing force is centrifugal force mω²r in the rotating frame. Both problems have the same FBD structure: horizontal normal force → vertical friction → supports vertical weight. The formula analog is a_min = g/μ (cart) vs. ω_min = √(g/μr) (rotor).

grounding_summary: Page 244 presents the Sticking of a Person with the Wall of Rotor. A person stands against the inner wall of a rotating drum (rotor). Centrifugal force mω²r provides the normal force N against the wall. Friction μN = μmω²r acts upward. For person not to fall: μmω²r ≥ mg, giving ω_min = √(g/μr). The page presents the derivation explicitly and connects it to the cart-block sticking result.

The textbook uses centrifugal force F_c in the derivation, noting that friction force (F) equals weight mg, and centrifugal force provides the reaction. This is the standard non-inertial frame treatment for NEET purposes.
grounding_excerpt: the person remains stuck to the wall even when the floor is removed, because friction force balances its weight in this condition.
By the law of conservation of energy
This is the minimum acceleration of the cart so that block does not fall.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Class 11 Part 1 — Chapter 5: Laws of Motion (Circular Motion and Friction)
sources.why_used: Primary NCERT source connecting friction, normal reaction, and circular motion equilibrium, foundational for the rotor sticking derivation.

sources.title: NEET Physics Class 11 Chapter 6 Friction Notes — ClassNotes.guru
sources.why_used: Verified NEET-targeted notes confirming the rotor sticking formula and FBD approach as standard NEET exam content.

review.notes: Content grounded on page 244 OCR. omega_min = sqrt(g/mu*r) derived. v_min = sqrt(g*r/mu) included. Mass-independence noted. r-dependence of both omega_min and v_min explained.
